text.title: django-api Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for django-api. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Choose DRF when the project requires a large ecosystem, complex permission hierarchies, or team familiarity with Django class-based views.
rule.rationale: DRF has 14 years of battle-testing, 1000+ third-party packages, built-in browsable API, and plugin stubs (django-stubs, djangorestframework-stubs) for mypy. Django Ninja has a smaller ecosystem (since 2021).

rule.statement: Choose Django Ninja for new greenfield projects that require native async, Pydantic v2 validation, or FastAPI-like ergonomics.
rule.rationale: Ninja's async path is first-class; adding async to DRF via adrf is bolted-on and produces sync-looking code that can silently not be async. Ninja is ~2-3x faster in high-concurrency benchmarks due to async I/O, not request overhead.

rule.statement: Never mix DRF and Ninja in the same Django app — URL conflicts and authentication context diverge.
rule.rationale: Lock the choice at project bootstrap. Mixing multiplies the failure surface and makes OpenAPI spec generation inconsistent.

rule.statement: Use ModelViewSet for standard CRUD operations on a single model with DefaultRouter URLs.
rule.rationale: ViewSets reduce boilerplate: list/create/retrieve/update/partial_update/destroy are generated from a queryset and serializer class. DefaultRouter handles URL naming automatically.

rule.statement: Use APIView for action verbs (activate, archive, retry) or endpoints that aggregate multiple models.
rule.rationale: APIView gives full control over HTTP method dispatch without forcing router URL patterns. Use when the endpoint doesn't map cleanly to a single model resource.

rule.statement: Use separate input serializers (validation) and output serializers (representation) — never one serializer for both.
rule.rationale: A single serializer that handles both input and output forces compromises: write-only fields mix with read-only fields, and changing one breaks the other. Separate serializers also make permission-based field differences explicit.

rule.statement: Never use Meta.fields = "__all__" on any user-facing serializer.
rule.rationale: New columns added to the model are automatically exposed to API consumers. This has caused accidental password hash, token, and internal ID leaks in production systems.

rule.statement: List fields explicitly in Meta.fields and declare read_only_fields for computed and auto-set fields.
rule.rationale: An allowlist prevents new columns from leaking. read_only_fields ensures id, uid, created_at, updated_at cannot be set via PATCH/PUT.

rule.statement: Use ModelSchema for output; use Schema (Pydantic) for input with explicit validators.
rule.rationale: ModelSchema auto-generates fields from the Django model for output, which is safe for read paths. Input schemas need explicit Pydantic validators to enforce business rules.

rule.statement: Configure simplejwt with 15-minute access tokens, 7-day refresh tokens, token rotation, and blacklisting enabled.
rule.rationale: Short access token lifetimes limit the blast radius of a leaked token. Rotation + blacklisting prevents refresh token replay after logout.

rule.statement: Store JWT tokens in HttpOnly cookies, not localStorage — or use dj-rest-auth for cookie middleware wiring.
rule.rationale: localStorage is accessible to JavaScript; XSS attacks can steal tokens. HttpOnly cookies are not accessible from JS and are sent automatically with same-origin requests.

rule.statement: Set IsAuthenticated as the global default; never set AllowAny as the default permission class.
rule.rationale: AllowAny as default means any new endpoint is publicly accessible until explicitly protected. A locked-down default forces explicit opt-in to public access.

rule.statement: Object-level permissions go in has_object_permission; permission_classes handles only route-level (method + auth) checks.
rule.rationale: has_permission runs before the object is fetched — cheap. has_object_permission runs after get_object() — allows ownership/org membership checks per resource.

rule.statement: Throttle by scope, not only anon/user. Add a burst scope for write endpoints and a login scope for auth endpoints.
rule.rationale: A single anon/user rate limit cannot differentiate between a safe read flood and a brute-force login attempt. Scoped throttling applies tighter limits where it matters.

rule.statement: Use cursor pagination only on indexed ordering fields (e.g., (-created_at, -id)); always paginate list endpoints.
rule.rationale: Cursor pagination on an unindexed field causes full table scans. The -id tie-breaker is required for stable ordering when multiple rows share the same created_at timestamp.
